questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a painless ulcer on the medial aspect of the left lower leg, just above the malleolus. The ulcer is shallow with a granulating base and irregular borders. There is surrounding brownish pigmentation and mild pitting oedema. She has varicose veins and a history of deep vein thrombosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The location (gaiter area), painless nature, irregular shallow shape, granulation tissue, surrounding lipodermatosclerosis and pigmentation (haemosiderin deposition) are classic for venous disease. The most tempting distractor is arterial ulcer, but arterial ulcers are typically painful, located on pressure points or toes, and have a punched-out appearance.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with a history of smoking, hypertension, and hypercholesterolaemia presents with a painful ulcer on the lateral aspect of the left foot, over the fifth metatarsal head. The ulcer is deep, well-demarcated, with a pale or necrotic base. He reports intermittent claudication. On examination, the foot is cool, pulses are absent, and there is marked pallor on elevation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The location (foot, pressure points), severe pain, absent pulses, claudication, and signs of ischaemia (pallor on elevation, coolness) point to arterial insufficiency. The most tempting distractor is diabetic neuropathic ulcer, but the patient has no history of diabetes, and neuropathic ulcers are typically painless and occur in areas of pressure overload, often with callus.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with known rheumatoid arthritis presents with a rapidly enlarging, extremely painful ulcer on the anterolateral aspect of the right lower leg. The ulcer has a characteristic violaceous, undermined border. The base is necrotic. She is afebrile and has no signs of systemic infection. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of rheumatoid arthritis (inflammatory bowel disease or arthritis association), rapid onset, severe pain, and the classic violaceous undermined border is diagnostic of pyoderma gangrenosum. The most tempting distractor is an arterial ulcer, but the lack of vascular risk factors, the undermined edge, and the associated autoimmune condition make pyoderma more likely.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old man of African-Caribbean origin with known sickle cell disease presents with a chronic ulcer on the medial aspect of the left ankle. The ulcer is shallow with a granulating base and mild surrounding hyperpigmentation. It has been present for 6 months and is moderately painful. He has had similar ulcers in the past. There is no history of varicose veins or DVT. Peripheral pulses are palpable. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The young age, ethnic background, known sickle cell disease, and recurrent ulceration at the ankle (medial malleolus) are classic for sickle cell leg ulcers. They can mimic venous ulcers but occur in younger patients without venous insufficiency. The most tempting distractor is venous ulcer, but the absence of varicose veins, lipodermatosclerosis, and the history of sickle cell disease point to the correct diagnosis.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with poorly controlled hypertension presents with a painful ulcer on the lateral aspect of the left lower leg, above the malleolus. The ulcer is shallow but has a necrotic eschar with a surrounding livedoid pattern (livedo reticularis). She has no varicose veins, no history of DVT, and peripheral pulses are present. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The location on the lateral leg (typical for hypertensive ulcers), severe hypertension, presence of livedo reticularis, and absence of peripheral arterial disease or venous insufficiency characterize Martorell ulcer. The most tempting distractor is arterial ulcer, but pulses are intact, and the livedo pattern is distinctive.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old woman presents with a shallow, irregular ulcer over the medial malleolus with surrounding lipodermatosclerosis. She has varicose veins. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the underlying cause?
questions.explanation: Duplex ultrasound is the first-line, non-invasive test to confirm venous incompetence by detecting reflux in superficial and deep veins. ABPI assesses arterial disease, not venous.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with type 2 diabetes and a clean, punched-out ulcer over the lateral malleolus has absent dorsalis pedis pulse. ABPI is 0.9. What is the best single test to rule out falsely elevated ABPI due to calcified vessels?
questions.explanation: In diabetes, medial arterial calcification can cause falsely normal ABPI. Toe pressures are less affected; a TBI <0.7 indicates peripheral arterial disease. Duplex is confirmatory but TBI is the simpler initial test.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old smoker presents with a painful, well-demarcated ulcer on the dorsum of the foot. He has a history of claudication. ABPI is 0.4. What is the correct interpretation of this result?
questions.explanation: ABPI <0.5 indicates severe peripheral arterial disease. Values 0.5–0.9 are moderate, >0.9 normal, and >1.4 suggests incompressible arteries.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a history of DVT presents with an ulcer in the gaiter area. On examination, there is haemosiderin staining. ABPI is 1.1. What is the next most appropriate investigation to assess the venous system?
questions.explanation: Venous duplex ultrasound is the gold standard for detecting venous reflux and obstruction. Handheld Doppler is less accurate; CT venography is for proximal DVT, not chronic venous insufficiency.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old male intravenous drug user presents with a deep, necrotic ulcer on the lower shin with a surrounding red halo. There are no varicose veins. ABPI is 1.2. What is the single most appropriate investigation to differentiate an arterial cause from other diagnoses?
questions.explanation: ABPI of 1.2 may be falsely elevated due to calcified vessels. Toe-brachial index is less affected and can unmask underlying arterial disease. Arterial duplex could follow but TBI is the simpler first step.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old woman presents with a shallow, painless ulcer above the medial malleolus, with surrounding lipodermatosclerosis and varicose veins. ABPI is 0.95. There is no sign of infection. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Compression bandaging is first-line for venous leg ulcers to reduce oedema and improve venous return. Debridement and dressings are adjunctive; antibiotics are not indicated without infection; elevation alone is insufficient.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with diabetes and a history of smoking has a deep, painful ulcer on the lateral malleolus. The foot is cold and peripheral pulses are absent. ABPI is 0.4. Which treatment is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Compression is contraindicated in severe arterial disease (ABPI <0.5) as it can worsen ischaemia. Revascularization, analgesia, smoking cessation, and debridement (with caution) are appropriate.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman has a leg ulcer with surrounding haemosiderin staining. ABPI is 0.7. The ulcer has slough and mild exudate. What is the most appropriate first step in management?
questions.explanation: An ABPI of 0.7 indicates mixed arterial-venous disease. Further arterial assessment (duplex) is needed before compression; reduced-pressure compression might still be risky without knowing the extent of arterial compromise.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a known venous leg ulcer develops spreading erythema, purulent discharge, and a temperature of 38.2°C. What is the most appropriate treatment?
questions.explanation: The patient has cellulitis, so systemic antibiotics (e.g., flucloxacillin) are first-line. Compression is deferred until infection resolves; debridement and dressings are adjunctive.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis on methotrexate develops a painful, punched-out ulcer on her shin. ABPI is 1.0. Skin biopsy shows vasculitis. What is the first-line treatment?
questions.explanation: Vasculitic ulcers due to rheumatoid arthritis require systemic immunosuppression; oral prednisolone is first-line for acute vasculitis. Compression may be used later but is not primary; increasing methotrexate is too slow; topical steroids are ineffective; pentoxifylline is not first-line.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old woman presents to the ED with a shallow, painless ulcer on her lower calf that has been present for 2 weeks. She has varicose veins and mild ankle oedema. Pedal pulses are palpable. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Obtain a wound swab for microbiology
questions.explanation: Before any compression therapy, it is essential to rule out arterial disease by measuring ABPI. A palpable pulse does not exclude significant arterial insufficiency, and compression could worsen ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 72-year-old smoker with known peripheral arterial disease presents with a painful ulcer on his great toe and rest pain. The foot is cool and pulses are absent. His ABPI is 0.4. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Arrange duplex ultrasound of the leg
questions.explanation: This is critical limb ischaemia (rest pain, ulcer, ABPI <0.5). Urgent vascular referral is mandatory for revascularisation. Compression is contraindicated. Duplex ultrasound may be part of the workup but must not delay referral.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with a chronic venous leg ulcer has an ABPI of 0.6. The ulcer is clean, shallow, and located on the medial gaiter area. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Prescribe analgesia and review in clinic
Refer to vascular surgery for assessment
questions.explanation: An ABPI between 0.5 and 0.8 suggests mixed arterial-venous disease. NICE recommends referral to a vascular specialist before any compression therapy, even reduced compression, to avoid exacerbating ischaemia.

questions.stem: A 58-year-old woman with a recently healed venous leg ulcer is being discharged from the dermatology clinic. She has no significant arterial disease. What is the most appropriate immediate next step to prevent recurrence?
options: Refer to a dietitian for weight management
Prescribe below-knee graduated compression stockings for life
Advise daily leg elevation for 2 hours
Start oral diuretics for mild oedema
questions.explanation: Lifelong compression stockings are the mainstay of recurrence prevention in venous leg ulcers. While other measures help, the single most effective and evidence-based action is compression therapy provided there is no arterial disease.

questions.stem: A 78-year-old woman with a 3-year history of venous ulceration presents with a change in the appearance of her ulcer over the past 8 weeks. The ulcer now has raised, rolled edges and a fungating base. There is no significant increase in pain or exudate. What single clinical finding is most concerning for a life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: Rolled, everted edges are classic for squamous cell carcinoma (Marjolin's ulcer) arising in a chronic venous ulcer. Granulation tissue indicates healing, lipodermatosclerosis and hyperpigmentation are chronic venous changes, and slough is non-specific; none represent the same malignant risk.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man with known peripheral arterial disease and a non-healing ulcer on his shin of 4 weeks' duration now reports severe pain in the foot even when resting. On examination, the foot is cool and pale, with reduced capillary refill. The ulcer itself is dry with a black eschar. Which clinical sign indicates that he has critical limb ischaemia requiring urgent vascular assessment?
questions.explanation: Dependent rubor (the foot turning red when lowered) is a sign of severe ischaemia and critical limb ischaemia. Ankle swelling is non-specific, foul discharge suggests infection, erythema may be cellulitis, and ulcer size alone does not indicate critical ischaemia; the rest pain and dependent rubor are key.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old man with poorly controlled type 2 diabetes presents with a painless ulcer on the sole of his foot, present for several weeks. Today, there is increased purulent discharge and surrounding erythema. The ulcer is deep and you can see the underlying tissue. What single finding at the bedside would be most indicative of underlying osteomyelitis?
questions.explanation: A positive 'probe to bone' test (using a sterile probe to feel hard bone at the ulcer base) has high specificity for osteomyelitis in a diabetic foot ulcer. Oedema, warmth, odour, and eschar are non-specific and can occur without bone infection.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation and a chronic venous leg ulcer presents with sudden onset of severe pain in the same leg 2 hours ago. On examination, the leg is pale, cold, and there are no palpable pulses below the femoral artery. The ulcer appears unchanged. What single finding is most indicative of a limb-threatening emergency?
questions.explanation: Loss of pulses in a cold, pale leg with sudden pain in an AF patient indicates acute limb ischaemia due to embolus, which is limb-threatening. Bulging varicosities are chronic, increased size is slow, blisters can occur but pulses are the key, and swollen calf suggests DVT (but pulses usually present).

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with long-standing venous ulceration and varicose veins is in the emergency department because his ulcer started bleeding suddenly. He is sitting in a chair and blood is dripping onto the floor. On examination, the ulcer is on the medial malleolus and there is a visible vein at the base. What single characteristic of the bleeding is most concerning and requires immediate action?
options: Blood that soaks one gauze per hour
questions.explanation: Spurting bright red blood indicates arterial bleeding, which is limb-threatening and requires immediate pressure and vascular referral. Venous bleeding is dark and oozing, clots easily, and soaks gauze slowly; pus indicates infection rather than haemorrhage.
